docs.title: Lens Service Notes
docs.text:
Personal reference for disassembly, cleaning, lubrication, and reassembly of camera lenses. Organized by manufacturer.

Contact Me

docs.title: Canon
docs.text: Lens Mount Notes FD 50mm f/1.4 SSC FD Classic fast fifty; haze and oil on aperture blades common

docs.title: Leica
docs.text: Lens Mount Notes Summar 50mm f/2 LTM M39 Collapsible; uncoated glass, prone to haze and oily blades

docs.title: Nikon
docs.text: Lens Mount Notes Nikkor 50mm f/1.4 AIS F (AI-S) Very serviceable; aperture and focus grease dry out over time

Name Email Message Send

docs.title: Canon 100mm f/2 LTM
docs.text:
I bougth this lens after consulation with Claude Opus 4.8. It pointed out that I had no long focal length lenses in my Canon LTM collection, so I figured I'd go for it.

This is a little longer than I'd normally shoot, and because it's 100mm instead of the normal 90 or 135, it doesn't match any normal Leica M framelines. As often as not, I adapt lenses to mirrorless cameras, most recently the Leica EV-1, so not a big problem. An anyway, I tend to use framelines as a rough guide, so I've been using it on my M10.

Cosmetically the lens was in great condition, but it had some nasty haze on an internal element, bad enough that the images were hazey messes. I'm all for a vintage look, but not when it's just caused by damage.

The aperture blades were in decent shape but look a tad rusty, which is never good for an aperture.

docs.title: Specifications
docs.text: Spec Value Focal length 100mm Max aperture f/2 Min aperture f/22 Elements / Groups 6 / 4 Aperture blades 13 Min focus distance 1.0m Filter thread 58mm Mount Leica Thread Mount (M39 × 1mm)

docs.title: Optical Design
docs.text:
There isn't a lot of information about this lens, but I did fine one writeup Pebble Place. He includes the design of the lens, which is a remarkably symmetic Gaussian design with 6 elements in 4 groups.

.

This breaks down as a schematic diagram, which I generated for my own edificaiton.

docs.title: Tools & Materials
docs.text:
Lenses of this vintage mostly involved flathead screws, which are a pain to deal with. Even worse, some of them are made of brass, which makes it extra-easy to destroy the slot in the screw.

My first piece of advice is to use screwdrivers that fit the slot in the screw very well, both in length and width. Fortunately my Vessel set of screwdrivers worked well, almsot to the point that it made me wonder if Vessel (a Japanese company) made them to match the screws of Japanese lenses of this vintage.

My second piece advice is to use rubber tools instead of lens spanners as much as possible. Lens spanners are almsot universally instruments of destruction. Get a good set of rubber gloves and a set or two of rubber lens cones of various sizes and use the whenenver possible. Lens spanners should be a last resort, and an act of desparation.

  • Small flathead screwdrivers (#000, #00)
  • Rubber friction tools or Rotatool for filter rings
  • Lens tissue and sensor swabs
  • 99.9% isopropyl alcohol
  • Lighter fluid (naptha) — for dissolving old grease and cleaning evaporated grease
  • Helicoid grease (e.g. Molykote EM-30L, EM-60L, or Helimax XP)
  • Rubber gloves
  • Cleaning solution (Eclipse and ROR)
  • Loupe or magnifier for element inspection (my favorite: Belomo aks Trilomo

docs.title: Step 1: Assess the lens
docs.text:
Before disassembly, examine the lens wide open against a light source.

My copy had pretty bad haze near the front of the lens.

There are two locations for haze: on one of the surfaces exposed to air, and the surface sandwiched between a doublet.

Haze on a surface has a fighting chance of being removable. Haze on the interface inside a double means the lens has to be separted, cleaned, and re-bonded, all of which is possible but very difficult, and requires special equipment.

We'll see as the service progresses that it was on the surface closest to the aperture. I could only be sure after I'd opened the lense and inspected the elements. I'm not sure why, but often if there are evaporated oil and grease deposits on a lens, they happen at this surface.

The lens itself was in pretty decent condition, given its age.

Looking through the lens, there was pretty strong haze somewhere inside th lens. Just looking through the lens I couldn't tell if it was on a surface, or if the haze was inside a doublet.

docs.title: Step 2: Unscrew the lens block from the focussing body
docs.text:
I used very sticky rubber gloves to unscrew the lens body from the helicoid (aka focussing) assembly. Mine was in pretty tight, but with firm force it came free. There is no set screw or lock ring holding it in place, so use firm force and believe in yourself.

It was clear at this point that the haze was on the surface of the rear element as I metnioned, the surface closest to the aperture blades. I have no idea why oil onlyh depoisted on this side and not the other.

I tried removing the haze with 99.9% isopropyl alcohol, acetone, naptha, ROR, Zeiss lens cleaner and Eclypse . The naptha made the biggest dent, but there was still significant haze on the surface. The coating was no doubt shot, but I could see from the other surfaces that the coatings were pretty light, so it wasn't a great loss, but I still had to get rid of the haze.

docs.title: Step 3: Remove the rest of the optical assembly
docs.text:
Start by removing the set screw in the front part of the lens. I used a 1.2mm Vessel flathead screwdriver, which fit perfectly.

With the screw removed, the front part of the lense block will unscrew from the rest of the block. With the front and back removed, you'll be left with just the aperture assembly.

![The front and rear components of the block removed](images/100mm-f2-ltm/IMG_7372.jpeg

docs.title: Step 4: Really dealing with the haze
docs.text:
Reluctantly, I prepared to polish off the haze, which also meant polishing off the a bit of the surface of the lens. This was inevitable.

There are a few services that can polish and re-coat a lens. I've sent one lens to a guy in Ukraine who did an excellent job re-coating the front element of a 50mm Elmar, with very haze to the haze pattern on this lens. Unfortunately, I wanted to finish this lens in a weekend, and it took several months to send the lens to Jaroslav, get it coated, and receive it back. There's also the possibility that the lens will get lost in transit, so I decided to use my home grown techniques for polishing the lens, developed over years of accidentally destroying lenses.

The main trick, I learned, is to create a polishing surface which matches the curvature of the lens surface being polished, and ideally about the same size, and then rotate the polishing surface gently but firmly with a polishing agent.

To create the polshing surface, I put a thin lens polshing cloth on the lens surface, then used a curing epoxy (in my case Sugru, which cures with humidition, but you can also use a UV-curing putty if you want to speed things up). I pushed the puttin into the lens to match the surface of the lens, then let the whole thing cure for 24 hours (a UV cure would be faster, of course). I then trimmed the polishing cloth to over-hang the now-curved surface of the hardened epoxy putty. The polishing cloth by this point was reasonably bonded to the epoxiy, forming a soft surfaced that matched the lens curvature.

I used 3 grades of cerium oxide, the same material used to polish scratches in car windshields. The coarsest was 3.5$\mu$m, then 2.5$\mu$m, and finally $1$\mu$m, all purchased on Amazon. I could have dipped the cloth directly in the polishing compound but found it easer to soak a bit of thick lens cleaning tissue (Pec pads work well) in the preapred polishing compound, then placed it on the lens cloth/epoxy putty tool.

And then I polished, trying to rotate the polishing tool to keep keep even pressure on the lens surface.

It took about an hour using the 3.5$\mu$m compound. I used my Belomo loupe to inspect the surface. By the way, the surface looked pockmarked, with an evenly-distrubted surface of raised dots, kind of like the condensation the outside glass of a cold drink. This further convinced me that I was dealing with hardened oil or or grease that somehow deposited on the lens.

docs.title: Step 3: Remove the rear element group
docs.text:
Remove the lock ring holding in the rear element gruop. There are two slots for a removal tool, but first try using hand force with sticky rubber gloves, and then a rubber tool if yuo have one that fits over.

The rear element will then slide out from the rest of the optics block.

docs.title: Step 4: Access and separate the helicoid
docs.text:
The focus helicoid is accessed from the front once the element group is removed. Mark the helicoid engagement point with a paint marker before separating — this is critical for recovering infinity focus on reassembly.

Unscrew the helicoid fully. Clean all old grease from the threads using lighter fluid on a cotton bud. Allow to dry completely.

Marking the engagement point

Use a small dot of paint pen on both the inner and outer helicoid threads at the point of separation. The helicoid must re-engage at the same thread start or infinity focus will be lost.

docs.title: Step 5: Clean aperture blades (if oily)
docs.text:
Inspect the aperture blades. If oily, carefully remove the aperture assembly. Wipe each blade with a cotton bud barely dampened with lighter fluid. Blades must be completely matte and dry before reassembly.

Avoid excess solvent near elements

Keep solvent away from cemented elements — it can wick under the cement and cause separation.

docs.title: Step 6: Clean optical elements
docs.text: Use lens tissue or a sensor swab with a drop of Eclipse. Clean from center outward in a spiral motion. Inspect under a loupe. Haze on internal surfaces sometimes responds to cleaning; coating separation or fungal etching does not.

docs.title: Step 7: Disassembly the focussing assembly
docs.text:
The front body of the lens rotates while focussing so I knew there would only be 2 helicoids in the assembly, one for the optics block, and one for the rangefinder coupling.

Start by putting the focus focus as infinity and measuring the overall length of the assembly. In my case it was 56.0mm. We'll be doing a lot more measurements so keep the calipers around.

I wasn't sure where to start, so I started with the 3 small flat-head screws on the knurled forcus ring, using a 1.2mm Vessel screwderivwer.

docs.title: Step 8: Verify
docs.text:
The Rangerfinder calibraiton seems off.

I confirmed the flange-to-rangefinder coupling distance is 7.50mm, exactly what it should be, but it looks

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This lens uses M39 screw mount but has rangefinder coupling — it will couple correctly on Leica M bodies via an M39-to-M adapter, but confirm the adapter passes the RF cam.
  • Several variants exist; earlier versions have a different front cosmetic treatment. Optical formula appears consistent across variants.
  • If infinity focus is slightly off after reassembly, rotate the front element group in its thread by very small increments — the thread pitch makes this a sensitive adjustment.

docs.title: Canon FD 50mm f/1.4 SSC
docs.text: A sharp, fast standard lens from Canon's FD mount era (1971–1987). The SSC ("Super Spectra Coating") version is the most desirable variant. Common service issues are fungus or haze on internal elements and oil migration onto the aperture blades.

docs.title: Specifications
docs.text: Spec Value Focal length 50mm Max aperture f/1.4 Min aperture f/16 Elements / Groups 7 / 6 Aperture blades 8 Min focus distance 0.6m Filter thread 52mm Mount Canon FD

docs.title: Tools & Materials
docs.text:
• JIS screwdrivers: #0, #1
  • Spanner wrench (adjustable)
  • Rubber friction tools (lens cap openers work well)
  • Sensor swabs and lens tissue
  • 99% isopropyl alcohol
  • Lighter fluid (Ronsonol) — for dissolving old grease
  • Helicoil grease (e.g. Molykote EM-30L or Nyogel 767A)
  • Aperture blade oil remover (lighter fluid on a cotton bud)
  • Cotton gloves
  • Cleaning solution (Eclipse or Zeiss wipes for elements)

docs.title: Step 1: Assess the lens
docs.text: Before disassembly, check the lens wide open on a light source. Note any haze, fungus, or dust. Stop down and confirm the aperture blades move freely and are oil-free.

docs.title: Step 2: Remove the rear mount
docs.text:
Using a JIS #1 screwdriver, remove the three screws securing the rear bayonet mount. Lift the mount off. The chrome aperture ring is now accessible.

JIS vs Phillips

These screws are JIS, not Phillips. Using the wrong driver will strip them. JIS screwdrivers have a slightly different tip angle.

docs.title: Step 3: Remove the aperture ring and rear group
docs.text: Unscrew the rear element group (it threads out counterclockwise). Set it aside on a clean surface element-side up. The aperture linkage arm will be visible once the rear group is removed.

docs.title: Step 4: Clean aperture blades (if oily)
docs.text:
If blades have oil on them, remove the aperture assembly. Dip a cotton bud in lighter fluid and gently wipe each blade. Let fully dry before reassembly. Blades should be completely matte black — any sheen indicates remaining oil.

Do not use excessive solvent

Too much solvent can seep behind elements or dissolve lacquered surfaces. Use a barely-damp bud.

docs.title: Step 5: Clean optical elements
docs.text: Use lens tissue or a sensor swab with a drop of Eclipse fluid. Clean in circular motions from center outward. Inspect with a loupe. Repeat until clear.

docs.title: Step 6: Regrease the helicoid
docs.text:
Remove the focus ring by unscrewing the set screw (under a cosmetic ring at the front). Separate the helicoids. Note the engagement point before separating — mark it with a marker if needed.

Clean all old grease with lighter fluid. Apply a thin coat of helicoid grease to the threads. Reassemble at the correct engagement point.

Engagement point

The helicoid must be re-engaged at the same thread start it came apart at, or focus infinity will be off. Mark the thread start with a Sharpie before separating.

docs.title: Step 7: Reassemble and verify
docs.text:
Reassemble in reverse order. Check:

  • [ ] Aperture blades snap cleanly at all stops
  • [ ] Focus throw feels smooth with no tight spots
  • [ ] No haze or cleaning marks visible on elements
  • [ ] Rear mount screws are snug
  • [ ] Infinity focus is accurate (check on a distant subject)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The SSC version has a chrome ring near the rear; the later S.C. version does not have this ring.
  • If infinity is slightly off after reassembly, the rear element group can be shimmed by rotating it slightly in its thread — very small adjustments have a noticeable effect.

docs.title: Leica Summar 50mm f/2
docs.text:
The Summar was Leica's first f/2 standard lens, produced from 1933 to 1940. It is a collapsible lens on the Leica Thread Mount (LTM/M39), and one of the most characterful lenses of the era — rendering with soft contrast, gentle falloff, and a distinct glow wide open. Nearly all surviving examples have some degree of haze; a clean one is rare.

Service is rewarding but requires patience. The glass is uncoated and extremely soft. "Summar haze" — a cloudiness within cemented element groups — is common and cannot be resolved by cleaning alone.

docs.title: Specifications
docs.text: Spec Value Focal length 50mm Max aperture f/2 Min aperture f/12.5 Elements / Groups 6 / 4 Aperture blades 10 Filter thread 36mm (A36 / Series V) Mount Leica Thread Mount (LTM/M39) Production 1933–1940 Design Collapsible

docs.title: Tools & Materials
docs.text:
• Spanner wrench (adjustable, fine-tipped for small rings)
  • Rubber friction grip
  • JIS screwdrivers: #00, #0
  • 99% isopropyl alcohol
  • Lighter fluid (Ronsonol) — for grease removal
  • Lens tissue and Eclipse fluid (or distilled water with a drop of dish soap)
  • Cotton gloves — mandatory; the uncoated glass shows every fingerprint
  • Soft cotton swabs
  • Helicoid grease (light viscosity — Molykote EM-30L or similar)
  • Loupe (10x minimum)

docs.title: Before You Start
docs.text:
Summar haze

The most common issue — a milky or oily cloudiness between the cemented rear elements — is not removable by surface cleaning. It is internal cement degradation. If present, the only remedy is professional re-cementing or sourcing replacement elements. Confirm whether haze is on a surface (cleanable) or internal (not) before disassembling.

Uncoated glass

The Summar's elements have no coating. The glass scratches extremely easily — far more so than modern coated lenses. Always use generous fluid and zero pressure. Never use dry tissue. One overzealous wipe can permanently damage an element.

docs.title: Step 1: Assess the lens
docs.text:
Extend the lens fully and hold it up to a bright light source. Look through from both ends at various angles. Map what you find:

  • Surface haze (on front or rear element face) — cleanable
  • Internal haze within a cemented group — not cleanable
  • Fungus — may be cleanable if caught early; etching is permanent
  • Oil on aperture blades — cleanable

Rack focus and feel for stiffness or gritty spots in the helicoid.

docs.title: Step 2: Remove the focus scale ring
docs.text: The knurled focus scale ring at the base of the lens barrel is retained by two or three small set screws. Loosen them (they may be under a lacquer seal — break it gently with a scalpel). Slide the ring off.

docs.title: Step 3: Remove the front element group
docs.text:
With the lens fully extended, the front element group unscrews counterclockwise. Use a rubber grip for friction. It may be tight from old lacquer or hardened grease. Once loose it will unthread smoothly.

Set it aside face-up on a clean surface.

Mark the thread position

Before removing, mark the barrel and front group with a felt marker so you can return it to the same rotational position. This preserves focus calibration.

docs.title: Step 4: Inspect and clean aperture blades
docs.text:
With the front group removed, the aperture blades are accessible. Use a loupe to check for oil. Oily blades appear shiny or stick when actuated.

If oily: dip a cotton swab barely in lighter fluid and gently wipe each blade. Let dry completely before proceeding. Blades should be uniformly matte black.

docs.title: Step 5: Remove the rear element group
docs.text:
The rear element group unscrews from the back of the barrel. Access it from the rear (camera-mount side). Use a spanner or rubber grip. Turn counterclockwise.

Handle with extreme care

The rear group contains the cemented elements most prone to Summar haze. Do not apply pressure to the glass. Hold by the metal cell only.

docs.title: Step 6: Clean the elements
docs.text:
Clean each exposed glass surface with lens tissue and a drop of Eclipse fluid. Use minimal pressure — let the fluid do the work. Inspect with a loupe after each pass.

For stubborn haze on a surface (not internal), a dilute solution of distilled water and a single drop of dish soap can help. Rinse with distilled water, then finish with Eclipse.

Do not clean internal cement surfaces

If a cemented group has internal haze, do not attempt to separate the elements. This requires optical cement and a UV curing lamp. Leave it to a specialist or accept it as-is.

docs.title: Step 7: Clean and regrease the helicoid
docs.text:
With both element groups removed, the helicoid is accessible. Clean old grease from the threads using lighter fluid on a swab. The original grease in these lenses is 80+ years old and will have turned waxy or gummy.

Apply a thin coat of fresh light helicoid grease. The Summar's helicoid is short — a light touch of grease is sufficient.

docs.title: Step 8: Reassemble
docs.text:
Reinstall the rear element group first, threading it in clockwise. Then reinstall the front group, aligning to your earlier mark if you made one.

Reinstall the focus scale ring, align the infinity mark, and tighten the set screws.

docs.title: Step 9: Verify
docs.text:
• [ ] Aperture blades are clean and snap cleanly through all stops
  • [ ] Focus ring moves smoothly with light, even damping through full travel
  • [ ] No haze, smears, or debris visible on any element surface
  • [ ] Lens collapses and extends without binding
  • [ ] Infinity focus is accurate on a distant subject

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Chrome and black-paint versions exist; the black-paint examples are early and rarer. Service is identical.
  • The non-standard minimum aperture of f/12.5 is characteristic and correct — not a miscalibration.
  • A hazy Summar still produces usable, often beautiful images. Many photographers prefer the soft wide-open rendering that mild internal haze can enhance. Don't over-service a lens that's producing results you like.
  • The Summar was succeeded by the Summitar (1939) and later the Summicron. Summitar service is similar.

docs.title: Leica Summilux-M 35mm f/1.4 (Pre-ASPH)
docs.text:
The Summilux-M 35mm f/1.4 pre-ASPH was produced from 1961 to 1994, when it was replaced by the current ASPH design. It is one of the most characterful wide-angle lenses in the M system — rendering with a distinctive veil and soft contrast wide open, transitioning to sharp, saturated results by f/2.8–f/4. The out-of-focus rendering, particularly the "cat's eye" bokeh from the oval aperture at wide stops, is considered part of its signature.

Two main variants exist: the early steel rim (1961–1963), which has no filter thread and is rarer and more collectible, and the standard production version (1963–1994), which takes E46 filters. Service procedure is similar between variants.

docs.title: Specifications
docs.text: Spec Value Focal length 35mm Max aperture f/1.4 Min aperture f/16 Elements / Groups 7 / 5 Aperture blades 12 Filter thread 46mm (E46) — steel rim variant has none Mount Leica M Production 1961–1994

docs.title: Tools & Materials
docs.text:
• Spanner wrench (adjustable)
  • Rubber friction grip
  • JIS screwdrivers: #00, #0
  • 99% isopropyl alcohol
  • Lighter fluid (Ronsonol) — for grease removal
  • Lens tissue and Eclipse fluid
  • Cotton gloves
  • Soft cotton swabs
  • Helicoid grease (medium-light viscosity — Molykote EM-30L or similar)
  • Loupe (10x minimum)

docs.title: Before You Start
docs.text:
Oil on aperture blades

The most common service issue on pre-ASPH Summiluxes is oil migration onto the aperture blades from a degraded or over-greased helicoid. Inspect the blades before disassembling anything — hold the lens against a bright light and actuate the aperture ring. Oily blades appear shiny or move sluggishly.

Know your variant

The steel rim version has a different front bezel construction. The filter thread on standard versions is E46 — confirm before ordering a replacement part or adapter. Early steel rim examples may have slightly different aperture blade counts or internal arrangement; verify before ordering replacement parts.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The pre-ASPH rendering wide open is intentional, not a flaw. Stopping down to f/2–f/2.8 cleans up contrast significantly while retaining the warm, three-dimensional character.
  • Infinity focus can shift after helicoid regrease. Verify on a distant subject and adjust the focus tab position if needed.
  • The ASPH version (1994–present) is a completely different optical design and is not interchangeable at the element level.

docs.title: Service Steps
docs.text:
The lens overlal was in good condition. The optical block just uscrews from the helicoid assembly, but in my copy the optical block assembly itself was loose because of a set screw that wasn't fully tightened. I managed to unscrew it, loosen the set screw in the optical assembly, clean up the aluminum shavings, then re-assemble the optical block and tighten the set screw. Not fun but it had to be done.

The helicoid assemlby was somewhat stiff, so the first task is figuring out how to get inside it.

docs.title: Nikkor 50mm f/1.4 AIS
docs.text: One of Nikon's most produced lenses, made from 1981 onward. Extremely well-built and straightforward to service. Common issues are dried focus grease (stiff or notchy focus ring) and dusty elements. The aperture blades rarely get oily unless the lens was stored poorly.

docs.title: Specifications
docs.text: Spec Value Focal length 50mm Max aperture f/1.4 Min aperture f/16 Elements / Groups 7 / 6 Aperture blades 7 Min focus distance 0.45m Filter thread 52mm Mount Nikon F (AI-S)

docs.title: Tools & Materials
docs.text:
• Phillips screwdrivers: #0, #1
  • Spanner wrench (adjustable, 30–50mm range)
  • Rubber friction grip
  • 99% isopropyl alcohol
  • Lighter fluid (Ronsonol)
  • Helicoid grease (Molykote EM-30L or similar)
  • Lens tissue and Eclipse fluid
  • Cotton gloves
  • Marker (to mark helicoid engagement point)

docs.title: Step 1: Assess the lens
docs.text: Mount on a body or hold to a light source. Look through at maximum aperture for haze, fungus, or separation. Rack focus full travel and note any stiffness. Stop down through all aperture stops.

docs.title: Step 2: Remove the rear mount
docs.text:
Three Phillips #1 screws secure the rear F-mount plate. Remove them and lift the plate off. The AI-S coupling and aperture lever are now visible.

Don't lose the AI follower spring

A small spring and ball bearing sit under the aperture coupling lever. They can fall out when the rear plate is removed. Place the lens on a tray before lifting the plate.

docs.title: Step 3: Remove the rear element group
docs.text: The rear element group is a threaded assembly that unscrews counterclockwise. Use a rubber grip for friction, or a spanner if it's tight. Set aside element-side up.

docs.title: Step 4: Separate the helicoids
docs.text:
Remove the three screws on the focus ring collar. The outer helicoid unscrews from the inner. Note or mark the engagement point before separating.

Clean all old grease from both helicoid threads with lighter fluid on a cotton swab. Let dry fully.

Apply a thin, even coat of fresh helicoid grease to the threads. Avoid applying too much — excess will migrate onto blades over time.

Grease amount

Less is more. A thin film covering the threads is sufficient. Heavy packing does not improve feel and creates migration risk.

docs.title: Step 5: Clean optical elements
docs.text:
Use lens tissue or a PEC-PAD with a drop of Eclipse. Clean each element surface. Check with a loupe at an angle to spot remaining smears.

The front element can be unscrewed with a rubber grip or spanner if it needs a thorough clean.

docs.title: Step 6: Reassemble and verify
docs.text:
Reassemble in reverse. Remember to seat the AI follower spring and ball before installing the rear plate.

Final checks:

  • [ ] Focus ring is smooth and damped through full travel
  • [ ] Aperture blades are clean and snap crisply
  • [ ] Elements are clear — no smears, haze, or debris
  • [ ] Rear mount screws are snug
  • [ ] Infinity focus is accurate

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The 50mm f/1.4 AIS is nearly identical internally to the 50mm f/1.8 AIS — service steps are the same.
  • Nikon used several different helicoid grease formulations over production life; the original grease dries and becomes waxy with age.
  • A well-serviced example should have a smooth, slightly damped focus feel — similar to a well-tuned German lens.
